title: AND23020 Managing Heart Failure
text_en: Symptoms in the yellow zone are warning signs. Call your doctor immediately if you gained 2-3 or more pounds in one day or 5 pounds in Have an increased shortness of breath. Have difficulty sleeping due to trouble breathing. Will persistent, fast or irregular heartbeats are feeling light-headed or as if you may faint.

title: AND23020 Managing Heart Failure
text_en: Red zone symptoms are emergencies that mean you need medical evaluation right away. Call 911 if you experience chest pain that lasts more than 15 minutes. Severe don going shortness of breath. Coughing up pink frothy foam or fainting.

title: AND23020 Managing Heart Failure
text_en: Staying on top of your symptoms and following your doctor's instructions can help you manage heart failure and reduce your trips to the hospital. To find out more about managing heart failure, talk to your health care practitioner.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: If you are having an operation, you may be given general anesthesia to push you to sleep and keep you free from pain. Your doctor may recommend general anesthesia for a procedure that is extensive, takes a long time, or requires you to be in an uncomfortable position.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Before your procedure, an IV line will be placed in a vein in your arm using a small tube called a cannula. The IV will deliver fluid and medications directly into your bloodstream. You may receive some medication to help you relax.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will be placed on the operating table and made as comfortable as possible.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: A blood pressure cuff will be placed on your arm to check your blood pressure readings.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Sticky pads will be placed on your chest to check your heart rate.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: And a clip will be put on your finger to check your body's oxygen levels.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: These devices allow the anesthesia specialist to closely monitor your vital signs before, during and after your procedure.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will begin receiving general anesthesia by either breathing anesthetic gases through a mask or through IV injection, which will cause you to fall asleep.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Once you are asleep, you will be given a mixture of oxygen and anesthetic gases either through your mask or through a special tube inserted through your mouth and into your windpipe. The tube is attached to a respirator, which helps you breathe while delivering the gases to your lungs.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Deep in your lung tissues, the gases are absorbed into your bloodstream and carried by blood cells to your brain. The anesthesia prevents your brain from receiving messages from nerves in your body. As a result, you will remain asleep and pain-free during your procedure, and you will have no memory of it when you wake up.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: After your operation, the anesthesia specialist will give you medications to reverse the anesthesia, and you will awaken quickly. If you had a breathing tube in place, he or she will remove it as soon as you can safely breathe on your own.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will be taken to the recovery room where you will be closely monitored, and given pain medication is needed. You may feel light-headed and slightly disoriented, but this feeling should pass quickly. Once you are stable, you will be sent to a hospital room or home.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: A surgeon will insert a pacemaker into your body if your heart's conduction system, the electrical system that makes your heart beat, is not working properly.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The heart is a muscular organ with four chambers. Blood collects in the heart's two upper chambers or atria before filling the two lower chambers or ventricles. In the ventricles contract, blood in the right ventricle is pumped to the lungs and blood in the left ventricle is pumped to the rest of the body.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The heart has a natural pacemaker called the Sinoatrial node or SA node. The pacemaker controls the heart rate by precisely determining when the muscles of the atria and ventricles contract. The electrical signal produced by the SA node reaches the ventricles through a narrow pathway that includes the so-called AV node and bundle branches. Taken together, these pathways are referred to as the conduction system of the heart.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Problems with the conduction system can result if the SA node malfunctions, causing the heart to beat too fast, too slow or irregularly. Alternatively, a blockage in the bundle branches can develop, causing the heart to beat more slowly or irregularly. These problems can be due to heart disease, medications, or cardiac surgery.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Before the procedure, your blood and urine will be tested. You will also have x-rays taken of your chest, and an electrocardiogram or EKG will record the electrical activity in your heart.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: A local anesthetic will be injected into your skin to numb the area where the pacemaker will be inserted.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The surgeon will make a small incision beneath your collarbone.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The pacemaker's leads will be inserted into the subclavian vein, and then threaded toward your heart until they reach the right atrium. Your surgeon will view x-ray images on a video screen to position the leads correctly.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Once the leads have been tested, they will be connected to the pulse generator.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The pulse generator will then be placed in a small pouch that your surgeon will create under the skin beneath your collarbone.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Your surgeon will then sew the incision back together.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: While you're in the hospital, your doctor will monitor your heart rate and blood pressure to be certain the pacemaker is working properly. Before you leave, he or she will make final adjustments to the pacemaker settings.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: After your procedure, over the counter pain medications such as acetaminophen or ibuprofen should be sufficient to relieve any pain. You will not be able to drive for about two weeks. Periodically, you will need to follow up with your doctor to test and adjust your new

title: ANH00012 Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
text_en: Your surgeon will perform an abdominal aortic aneurysm repair to prevent the rupture of a bulging area of your aorta.

title: ANH00012 Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
text_en: The largest artery in the body is the aorta, which is attached to the heart and receives blood directly from the left ventricle or main pumping chamber. The aorta is divided into four parts, the ascending aorta, aortic arch, thoracic aorta, and the abdominal aorta.
